ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general milk tea entry; no specific Thai green milk tea entry exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Thai green milk tea code. A0FKP (tea-based beverages with milk) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Thai green milk tea. Not fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Thai green milk tea is approximately 350 g, as commonly served at Thai beverage stalls and bubble tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Green Milk Tea — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database at confidence 0.95. Cholesterol (5.71 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.57 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) were flagged in the input record as originating from a delivery platform source at confidence 0.70; these three values are retained as the best available estimate but are noted as pending verification against an approved primary source (HPB or USDA FoodData Central) in a future review cycle. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and most micronutrients are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened green tea with milk — analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI data for tea-based milk beverages with added sugar
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Thai green milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 31 (low category) is estimated by analogy to sweetened green tea with milk beverages documented in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al., Am J Clin Nutr). The input record cited GI 31 from an internal embedded GI database at confidence 0.316; this value is consistent with the low-GI range for sweetened tea-milk beverages and is retained as a defensible analogous estimate referenced to the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (31 × 26.6) / 100 = 8.25, rounded to 8.4 for a 350 g standard cup (7.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 = 26.6 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.6 g sugar per 100 g; added condensed milk contributes saturated fat and concentrated sugar
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage (7.6 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold); habitual SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018) and should be replaced with unsweetened green tea or water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage; 7.6 g sugar per 100 g from condensed milk and added sugar; rapid glycaemic impact of liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid Thai green milk tea entirely, as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 26.6 g of sugar per standard 350 g cup; liquid sugars raise postprandial blood glucose rapidly and should be replaced with plain unsweetened green tea brewed without milk or sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.57 g per 100 g from condensed milk and creamer; added sugar 7.6 g per 100 g; trans fat 0.07 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Thai green milk tea due to its saturated fat content from condensed milk (1.57 g/100 g), small trans fat contribution (0.07 g/100 g), and high added sugar load (7.6 g/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and endothelial function; unsweetened green tea without dairy is the preferred alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and saturated fat from condensed milk promote pro-inflammatory mediators; green tea polyphenols (EGCG) are anti-inflammatory but negated by the dairy and sugar matrix
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Thai green milk tea as the added sugar and condensed milk promote systemic inflammation, negating the anti-inflammatory catechin benefit of green tea; plain unsweetened green tea is recommended instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai green milk tea (ชาเขียวนมไทย, romanized: Cha Khiao Nom Thai) is a Thai-style sweetened beverage made by brewing green tea leaves and combining the infusion with condensed milk, evaporated milk or non-dairy creamer, and sugar, then serving chilled or over ice. The drink is characterised by a mildly grassy, slightly sweet, and creamy flavour profile with a smooth, light-bodied texture. It is widely sold at Thai beverage stalls, bubble tea shops, and hawker centres across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Per 100 g, Thai green milk tea provides approximately 46 kcal, 0.5 g protein, 1.51 g fat (of which 1.57 g is saturated fat from dairy), and 7.6 g carbohydrate (all as sugar). Sodium is low at 19 mg per 100 g. A standard 350 g cup delivers approximately 161 kcal and 26.6 g of sugar. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high added sugar content, saturated fat from condensed milk, and trace trans fat. The green tea component provides catechins (EGCG) with anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the sugar and dairy matrix. The glycemic index is estimated at 31 (low category) by analogy to sweetened green tea with milk beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycemic load per standard serving is 8.4, which is clinically meaningful for glycaemic management.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from HPB Singapore at confidence 0.95, qualifying as an exact approved-source match. However, cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were originally tagged to a delivery platform source in the input record at confidence 0.70 and have not been independently verified against an approved primary source; this lowers overall record confidence to medium. GI is an analogous estimate rather than a direct measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Thai green milk tea is not reported in the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this beverage entry. 3. Cholesterol (5.71 mg/100 g), saturated_fat_g (1.57 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.07 g/100 g) were sourced from a delivery platform (GrabFood) in the original input record at confidence 0.70; these values are retained as the best available estimate but should be verified against an approved primary source (HPB or USDA FoodData Central) in a future review cycle. 4. GI value of 31 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened green tea with milk beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct Thai green milk tea GI entry was found. 5. The input record listed two portions both flagged is_default=true (1 jumbo cup 700 g and 1 standard cup 350 g); the standard cup (350 g) is used as the serving reference for GL calculation. 6. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are noted; low_sodium is supported (19 mg/100 g) but 'low_calorie' is a relative descriptor and is not carried forward as a clinical claim given the high sugar content. 7. Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source URL updated from the Foodo sub-portal to the canonical HPB food information and database URL to ensure approved-source compliance. GI source organisation and URL confirmed as University of Sydney GI Database (https://www.glycemicindex.com/) with analogous_estimate match type explicitly documented.

Iteration 3: Lowered confidence from 'high' to 'medium' to accurately reflect that cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat remain unverified against an approved primary source.
